title: Quick check: MX priority and targets

questions.prompt: A new client onboarding sets up two mail providers as MX records: their primary at priority 30 and their backup at priority 10. Which one will sending servers actually try first?
choices: The primary at 30 — higher priority number means higher preference.
The backup at 10 — but that's the opposite of what they intended, because lower priority number is preferred. The records need to be swapped (primary at 10, backup at 30).
Whichever the receiver's DNS cache returns first.
questions.explanation: Lower priority = higher preference (tried first). The 'primary at 30 / backup at 10' setup inverts the intent; sending servers will try the backup first and only fall through to the primary if the backup is unreachable. Swap the numbers.

questions.prompt: A vendor's setup instructions ask you to publish an MX record pointing at `198.51.100.42` (an IP address). Should you?
choices: Yes — modern receivers accept IP-as-MX even though the spec discourages it.
No. MX targets must be hostnames with A/AAAA records, not IP addresses. Ask the vendor for a hostname; reputable mail providers publish one.
Yes — wrap the IP in square brackets for the IP-literal form.
Only if the IP is in the same /24 as the receiver's network.
questions.explanation: RFC 5321 requires MX targets to be hostnames. Some DNS hosts let you save the IP form; many receivers reject and the mail bounces. You don't paper over vendor sloppiness with broken DNS.

questions.prompt: MX records can safely point at a CNAME, as long as the CNAME resolves to a host with A/AAAA records.
questions.explanation: RFC 2181 prohibits MX-pointing-at-CNAME. Some receivers follow the chain; others reject. The defensive practice is to point MX directly at the canonical FQDN that has A/AAAA records, not via a CNAME indirection.
